title: Kubernetes Pod Networking Deep Dive
description: Explain how pod-to-pod communication works in Kubernetes — CNI plugins, overlay networks, and Service routing. Visual walkthrough with a live cluster.

demoItems.description: Spin up a 3-node kind cluster and trace packets between pods using tcpdump
kubectl exec into pods and curl across namespaces to show Service DNS resolution

talkingPoints.text: Every pod gets its own IP — no NAT between pods
CNI plugins are swappable — Calico, Flannel, Cilium each solve it differently
Subscribe for the upcoming Service Mesh video

shootingScript:
1. Intro — why networking is the #1 confusing topic in K8s
2. Whiteboard: pod IP assignment, node CIDR ranges
3. Demo: kind cluster, deploy two pods, curl between them
4. Explain CNI plugin role with Calico example
5. Service abstraction — ClusterIP, kube-proxy, iptables
6. Demo: create a Service, trace DNS resolution
7. Recap and next video teaser (Service Mesh)
thumbnailIdeas: Split screen: left side shows a tangled network diagram, right side shows clean pod-to-pod arrows. Text overlay: "How Pods Actually Talk"
notes: Keep the CNI comparison short — viewers drop off during theory-heavy sections. Focus on the live demo.

learnings.text: Live terminal demos need larger font size (at least 18pt) — got comments about readability
Whiteboard diagrams before live demos help viewers follow along — retention was higher in sections with visual prep

feedback.text: Best networking explanation I've seen — the live demo made it click
Should have shown a network policy example — missed opportunity to connect to security

title: Terraform State Management — Remote Backends & Locking
description: Walk through why local state breaks in teams, how to configure S3 + DynamoDB backend, and state locking to prevent corruption.

demoItems.description: backend.tf with S3 bucket and DynamoDB table configuration
Simulate a state lock conflict by running terraform apply from two terminals

talkingPoints.text: Local state is a ticking time bomb for teams — one overwrite and you're in trouble
DynamoDB locking is cheap (pennies/month) and prevents state corruption
If this saves you from a state corruption incident, drop a like

shootingScript:
1. Intro — "I've seen teams lose entire infrastructure state files"
2. What is Terraform state and why it matters
3. Demo: local state, show the terraform.tfstate file
4. Problem: two engineers run apply at the same time
5. Solution: remote backend with S3
6. Demo: configure backend.tf, run terraform init -migrate-state
7. Add DynamoDB locking, demo the lock conflict
8. Best practices: state per environment, encryption at rest
thumbnailIdeas: Broken lock icon with Terraform logo. Text: "Your State File Is Not Safe"
notes: Recording done. Need to edit out the section where the AWS credentials expired mid-demo. Re-recorded that part separately.

title: Building a CLI Tool with Go and Cobra
description: Step-by-step guide to building a production-quality CLI tool in Go using Cobra. Cover subcommands, flags, config files with Viper, and cross-compilation.

demoItems.description: github.com/example/task-cli — sample task manager CLI built with Cobra
go build with GOOS/GOARCH for cross-compilation to Linux and macOS

talkingPoints.text: Cobra is used by kubectl, Hugo, GitHub CLI — it's the industry standard
Viper + Cobra together give you flags, env vars, and config files for free

shootingScript:
1. Why Go for CLI tools (single binary, fast startup, cross-compile)
2. cobra-cli init — scaffold the project
3. Add a 'task add' subcommand
4. Add flags: --priority, --due
5. Wire up Viper for config file (~/.task-cli.yaml)
6. Add 'task list' with table output
7. Cross-compile and test on Linux via Docker
8. Recap: Go + Cobra is the fastest path to a real CLI tool
thumbnailIdeas: Terminal screenshot with a polished CLI output. Cobra snake logo in the corner. Text: "Build CLIs Like a Pro"
notes: Script is ready. Need to finalize the demo repo and verify cross-compilation steps before recording.

title: Docker Multi-Stage Builds for Production
description: How to use multi-stage builds to create minimal, secure production images. Compare image sizes, layer caching strategies, and security scanning.

demoItems.description: Dockerfile with multi-stage build — builder stage + distroless runtime

talkingPoints.text: A typical Node.js image is 1GB+ — multi-stage gets you under 100MB

thumbnailIdeas: Before/after image size comparison. Giant container vs tiny container.
notes: Idea captured. Research distroless vs Alpine for the comparison section.

title: GitOps with ArgoCD — From Zero to Auto-Deploy
description: Set up ArgoCD on a Kubernetes cluster and configure auto-sync from a Git repo. Cover app-of-apps pattern, health checks, and rollback.

demoItems.description: ArgoCD installation on kind cluster with port-forwarding to the UI
gitops-demo repo with Kustomize overlays for dev/staging/prod

talkingPoints.text: GitOps means Git is the single source of truth — no more kubectl apply from laptops
ArgoCD auto-sync + self-heal = your cluster always matches what's in Git

thumbnailIdeas: ArgoCD UI screenshot showing a healthy app tree. Git logo with an arrow to a K8s cluster.
notes: Technical research done. ArgoCD v2.14 has a new multi-source feature — should I cover that or keep it focused on basics? Basics first, advanced in a follow-up.
